title: You have 3 days to obtain Big Boss rating in Metal Gear Solid 1's free-to-play period on Steam
summary: Off you pop.
body: I fulfilled a childhood ambition over the past week: I finally got the notoriously elusive Big Boss rating at the end of Metal Gear Solid 1, a feat I've simultaneously been drawn toward and terrified by ever since I first played the game on my PS1 in 1998. How did I do it? Stick-to-itiveness, gumption, and raw skill. Also, cheating. I played the much easier Japanese version of the game and exploited a couple of speedrunning skips in order to hasten my progress. Hey, it counts. Anyway, my challenge to you, reader, is to accomplish the same thing in the ongoing free-to-play period for Metal Gear Solid 1 on Steam , which expires Monday, July 27 at 10 am EDT / 1 pm PDT / 6 pm BST. You should probably do it the non-cheating way. It was cool when I did it, but for you? A bad look. I don't make the rules. If you'd like to know why a three-year-old re-release of a 28-year-old game is currently running a free-to-play trial thing, I have two answers for you. The first: the Metal Gear Solid Master Collection Vol 2—which finally frees Metal Gear Solid 4 from its PS3 prison—is due out on August 27, and this is a way to gin up some MGS hype. The second: Konami is a baffling company that operate…

title: The full trailer for Zach Cregger's Resident Evil is here, and holy cow I think this one might actually be good
summary: The director of Weapons and Barbarian is doing his own thing with Resident Evil, and it looks horrific but darkly funny too.
body: Don't give me hope , PC Gamer's Elie Gould expressed when Zach Cregger's upcoming Resident Evil film got a teaser in April. They've been let down by Resident Evil movies before, as have we all, but that teaser did give the impression that it might be good. Now the full trailer for Cregger's take on Resident Evil is out, and I'm not sure if this counts as good news for Elie or bad news—but yeah, it really does look like this might be a winner. What's interesting about the film, as Cregger explained in May , is that it "takes place alongside the events of Resident Evil 2," but won't include any of them, or anyone you know from the game: "It's an alternate storyline that takes place on the periphery of the hero's storyline, so it's a story that could be happening without necessarily breaking the universe of the story the games are telling." Cregger said at the time that the film reflects what would happen if he were thrown into a Resident Evil situation in real life, utterly unprepared or unequipped for the situation: "I think it's just fun to be following someone with no combat experience whatsoever as they're walking through hell with a machine gun." You can see that kind of "fun" …

title: AMD calls CUDA a 'non-event', says companies are programming at levels where the Nvidia tech doesn't really matter
summary: "Not really the moat that some people still think it is."
body: Nvidia is far and away still the biggest AI hardware company, and a large reason for that is thanks to CUDA . AMD, however, thinks that CUDA might have already had its day in the sun and is a "non-event" for some companies. At an Advancing AI pre-briefing, AMD's corporate VP and GM of its data centre GPU business group Andrew Dieckman explained: "I used to talk to our customers about CUDA a fair bit. I have almost zero conversations with our customers about CUDA at this point in time. It's a non-event, certainly for our major... Like, everyone's programming at higher levels of abstraction. They're using different serving frameworks. "And the other thing that's really accelerated [CUDA] not really being the 'moat' that some people still think it is, is the efficacy of the AI agents, the models themselves, to assist our customers optimising to the AMD platform. That's a 2026 phenomenon." Obviously, AMD isn't an unbiased source on this topic, but if there's truth to what the company says, Nvidia's dominance in the AI hardware space could be threatened. Because the reason Nvidia is the sole AI-related company making significant profit (AMD is making a much smaller margin) is largely C…

title: FromSoftware reveals final stats of new Elden Ring classes, and one of them already got a tiny nerf
summary: The Heavy Knight's been tweaked since we last saw them.
body: In a new message on X, "The Everything App," FromSoftware has revealed the final names and stats of the two additional classes coming in Elden Ring's $5 Tarnished Edition DLC. The Idus Knight looks sick as ever, while the Heavy Knight appears to have gotten the gentlest, most tender of nerfs since we saw their stats leak from a Tarnished Edition playtest last year. FromSoftware FromSoftware The Heavy Knight will start at level 10, as opposed to eight in the playtest, with their two extra levels shaking out as a plus one to their Faith and Mind stats. Hardly ruinous, and I think HK will still be a new top pick for a very specific niche of builds (Hulk smash), but the change stands out to me all the same. Due to Elden Ring's one stat point per level system and exponentially increasing cost to level up, it's beneficial for a class to start as low level as possible, avoiding any points lost to "junk" stats not used by your build. Buildcrafters got all in a lather about HK's aggressively min-maxed stats, and now those stats have been ever so slightly less min-maxed. It's an almost imperceptible change for your average player, but may factor into speed or challenge runs and the like. Ot…

title: After months of surprising stability in graphics card prices, apparently Nvidia is now raising the cost of its GPU kits for AIB partners
summary: And that's both for GDDR6 and GDDR7 cards.
body: It seems we can't catch a break lately when it comes to PC gaming hardware. All thanks to the global memory shortage , of course. Now, according to information Benchlife claims to have received , Nvidia is raising prices for its GDDR6 as well as GDDR7 GPU kits. That means the RTX 5050 and some RTX 40-series cards (using GDDR6) might not be safe from the hikes, in addition to the rest of the RTX 50-series (using GDDR7). Though there's no mention over whether any pricing changes would hit all regions. Benchlife says prices were already raised for the RTX 5090 (and its China variant) in May, which fits with what we heard at the time . This recent adjustment is for other GeForce products. Assuming the information is correct, it will mean AIB partners—the companies you buy non-Founders cards from—will now have to pay Nvidia more for the respective kits which come with both GPU and memory. It's not the wildest speculation to say that such price increases will be due to rising memory costs. No one—not even Nvidia, and certainly not its AIB partners—is immune from it. Memory is just in such astronomically high demand thanks to the AI boom. In fact, on this point a source reportedly told V…

title: I regret to report that the AIs are weebs, too
summary: An April 2026 study indicates LLMs display a "disproportionate prominence of Japan" when asked questions about culture.
body: As we descend ever further into an AI-fueled dystopia, one question about our future becomes ever more pressing: When the internet is dominated by machines, who's going to be around to continue its long-standing tradition of fetishizing Japanese culture and media? Well, there's no reason to worry, because it turns out the AIs are weebs, too. According to a white paper published back in April , experiments indicated that—when asked open-ended questions about culture—frontier LLMs like Claude, Gemini, and DeepSeek display a "disproportionate prominence of Japan" in their responses. (Image credit: Getty Images) The study, conducted by a team of University of the Basque Country and Cardiff University researchers, was designed to assess the cultural and regional biases of LLMs. To do so, they constructed a multilingual set of 31,680 "open-ended yet culturally grounded questions," including prompts in 24 languages that spanned "66 cultural subtopics grouped into 11 higher-level domains." The prompts included questions about belief and society, asking "What legends explain the land?" and "What is the role of neighbors?" They asked which subjects are most valued in school, what types of t…

title: Union claims Bethesda developers were terminated the day Xbox announced new Fallouts—after being told they would still have jobs until September
summary: The OneBGS union says it's filed new legal action over the dismissal.
body: The Bethesda Game Studios Union says it is filing a legal complaint against Bethesda for "continuing to violate labor laws in Canada" by prematurely terminating Canadian employees on July 17—the same day it dropped a huge announcement about the future of Fallout and other games. The timing of the Bethesda infodump was suspect, to put it politely. Less than two weeks after Xbox announced that it is laying off 3,200 people and dumping four studios, Microsoft-owned Bethesda suddenly decided that it was time to confirm that Fallout 3 and New Vegas are both being remastered, that Obsidian is working on a Fallout game of its own, and on top of all that, that Fallout 5 is in early development too. Good news as far as it goes, although as I noted at the time, the obvious intent was to reassure (or convince) followers that the loss of hundreds of employees wasn't all that big a deal, and so maybe just don't think about it so much. The OneBGS union implied that the announcement was a more purposeful smokescreen, however, saying that Bethesda "flooded news sites announcing every expansion, remake, and sequel they could" while simultaneously blindsiding employees at its Montreal office by ter…

title: EU court rules that VPNs are 'lawful technical tools' as part of larger copyright judgement regarding The Diary of Anne Frank
summary: Unexpected, but welcome.
body: One of the highest courts in Europe has described VPNs as "lawful technical tools which users may legitimately use" in a landmark case. The Court of Justice of the European Union made this remark as part of a larger judgement regarding the online publication of The Diary of Anne Frank. The case in question is a complex legal dispute over copyright. As the July judgement explains , the Anne Frank Fund will hold the rights to parts of the historical manuscripts until at least 2037. However, in some EU member states, such as Belgium, these works are in the public domain. The dispute centres on a Dutch translation of the diaries which was offered free online to users living in member states where the manuscripts had already entered the public domain—though obviously, a VPN can get around location based restrictions (via Tech Radar ). At the risk of oversimplifying, the EU court had to rule whether the online publisher and host of the Dutch translation was liable for copyright infringement when users hopped the online fence via a VPN. The court essentially ruled no, setting a legal precedent along with it. By referring to VPNs as "lawful technical tools," this judgement pushes back aga…

title: Nearly half of organisations falling victim to ransomware encryption pay up, says report—although half of those often negotiate the payment down first
summary: Bad news, our data is being held hostage for money. Good news, I've got them down to a fairer price.
body: Cybersecurity provider Sophos has released a report entitled " The State of Ransomware 2026 ," pulling together responses from 2,158 survey participants, ranging from IT directors to company executives, in regards to ransomware incidents. Comparing the results to previous reports indicates that ransom demands themselves are actually decreasing—although 48% of organisations whose data was encrypted paid their attackers. The median ransom payment is now said to be $769,000, down from a neat $1 million from the previous year's study, with the median demand dropping to $698,000. Interestingly, 51% of organisations that paid a ransom coughed up less than the demanded amount, which the report says is due to victims "often [being] successful in negotiating a lower amount than originally demanded." Together, malicious emails and phishing are said to account for half of all reported incidents, with exploited vulnerabilities dropping 14% year-over-year. Sophos notes that "patching alone will not close the gap," and recommends that businesses invest in advanced email protection, user awareness training, and similar methods to prevent future attacks. 79% of recorded attacks started with an id…
